title: Microsoft's $50bn Data Centre Push to Consume 500,000 Tonnes of Copper Over Five Years
description: Microsoft's $50bn Data Centre Push to Consume 500,000 Tonnes of Copper Over Five Years The Australian
htmlContent:
Microsoft's $50bn Data Centre Push to Consume 500,000 Tonnes of Copper Over Five Years

By Cameron England, The Australian • June 6, 2026

SYDNEY – Microsoft's five-year, $50 billion data centre expansion programme will require approximately 500,000 tonnes of copper, according to analysis by commodities consultancy Wood Mackenzie, putting stark numbers on the commodity demand implications of the artificial intelligence infrastructure race.

The figure — equivalent to about 2.2% of current annual global copper production — relates only to Microsoft's declared capital programme and does not account for equivalent investments by Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, Meta and other hyperscale operators, each of which has announced multi-year expansion plans of comparable or greater scale.

"When you aggregate the copper requirements of all the major hyperscalers, you are looking at a demand increment that dwarfs anything the mining industry has previously had to absorb from a single end-use sector in such a short time," said Wood Mackenzie analyst Eloise Prior.

Copper is critical to data centre construction and operation. Power distribution systems, transformers, busbars, liquid cooling loops and the dense wiring of server racks all rely heavily on the metal. A single hyperscale campus of 100 megawatts — a mid-size facility by current standards — can require between 6,000 and 10,000 tonnes of copper, according to industry estimates.

Microsoft has committed to powering all its data centres with 100% renewable energy by 2030, a goal that will itself drive further copper demand through onsite solar, wind and battery storage installations, as well as the transmission grid upgrades needed to deliver that power reliably.

Australian copper miners including OZ Minerals — now BHP Copper Australia — and Sandfire Resources have both highlighted the technology sector's growing role as a direct offtake customer in recent investor presentations. BHP said last week it had received expressions of interest from US and Asian technology companies seeking long-term copper supply agreements.

The news added fresh momentum to copper prices, which remain near three-month highs above $10,900 per tonne on the LME. Australian copper stocks also rallied, with Sandfire adding 4.8% and 29Metals gaining 6.1% on the ASX.

Tags: Microsoft, copper, data centre, AI, Wood Mackenzie, BHP, Sandfire, demand, infrastructure

Sentiment signal: bullish
